title: Installing an outdoor tap
subtitle: Connecting an outside tap to the rising main with an approved backflow preventer
excerpt: An outdoor tap makes watering from a hose practical and avoids the indoor trip with watering cans. The installation requires a connection to the cold-water rising main, a double-check valve for backflow protection (required under Water Supply (Water Fittings) Regulations 1999), and a stop valve for winter isolation. A basic installation costs 50 to 80 pounds in materials and takes 3 to 4 hours.
sourceNotes: Water Supply (Water Fittings) Regulations 1999 (England and Wales) on backflow prevention. WRAS (Water Regulations Advisory Scheme) guidance on outside taps. CIPHE guidance on domestic plumbing additions.

glossaryTerms.definition: A container for collecting rainwater from a roof downpipe for garden use. Typical capacity 100 to 500 litres.

content.text: Under the Water Supply (Water Fittings) Regulations 1999, you must notify your water supplier before connecting a new tap or fitting to the supply. Most suppliers accept notification online and do not require inspection for a standard outside tap with a double-check valve. Fitting without notification can result in disconnection.
An outdoor tap reduces the time and effort of garden watering and can be connected to a hose for a
water butt

top-up during dry periods. The critical regulatory requirement is the double-check valve, also called a backflow preventer. It prevents contaminated garden water from siphoning back into the drinking supply when the hose end is left submerged. Fitting a tap without a double-check valve is a Water Fittings Regulations offence.
What you will need
Outside tap kit: includes the tap, double-check valve, isolating valve, wall plate elbow, copper pipe (15 mm, 300 mm length), and compression fittings
15 mm pipe cutter
25 mm masonry bit for the wall core
PTFE tape for the threaded connections
A length of 15 mm copper pipe from the rising main to the external wall, as required by the distance
Step by step
Turn off the water supply at the mains stopcock. Open a cold tap to drain the rising main.

Locate the rising main (the cold supply pipe from the mains entry, usually in a cupboard under the stairs or in the kitchen). Plan the route to the external wall. Keep bends to a minimum and ensure the pipe does not freeze where it passes through an unheated space.

Cut into the rising main with a pipe cutter at a convenient point. Fit a tee fitting with a 15 mm branch. Connect the isolating valve to the branch, then the double-check valve. The arrow on the valve body must point toward the tap, not back to the main. Then run the pipe to the external wall.
Drill through the external wall with a 25 mm masonry bit, angled very slightly downward to allow drainage. Feed the pipe through the core.
Fix the wall plate elbow to the external wall at the pipe exit point. Connect the tap to the wall plate elbow. Seal around the pipe core with a waterproof flexible sealant to prevent damp ingress.
Turn the mains stopcock on. Close the outdoor tap and open the isolating valve. Check all joints for drips. Allow 30 minutes, then re-check.
Winter isolation

Before the first frost, close the indoor isolating valve, open the outdoor tap to drain the external pipe, then close the tap. This prevents freeze damage to the exposed section. In Scotland and at altitude above 200 m, this winterisation should be done by October; in southern England, December is typically safe.

attrs.symptom: Drip from the tap when fully closed

content.text: The tap seat has limescale or a worn washer. Isolate the tap, remove the headgear with a spanner, replace the rubber washer. Descale the seat with a cloth and limescale remover if necessary.

attrs.symptom: Reduced flow compared to indoor taps

content.text: Check whether the double-check valve is partially closed. Some types have an integral strainer that blocks with debris; remove and clean the strainer insert.
